D7 Print L5 Evaluate Reference

UV-Curing Acrylate Ink

a UV photon kicks a photoinitiator into a radical, which yanks a neighbour into the chain — and a liquid film sets solid in milliseconds

UV ink is liquid acrylate monomer plus a photoinitiator. A UV photon hits the initiator and produces a reactive radical (briefly red). The radical bonds to a neighbouring monomer, leaving that neighbour now reactive — the chain propagates outward, locking monomers into a polymer network. Stop the lamp and the reaction halts almost at once.

Switch the UV lamp on. Watch fresh activations flash red, then darken into polymer, and the chains spread between them. Raise the intensity to fire more photons per second; the cure completes faster and the network is denser.
